candidates.description: From the Master Metaphor List / Osaka archive. Maps something moving toward you onto time.

candidates.name: TREATING ILLNESS IS FIGHTING A WAR
candidates.archive_ref: Osaka archive: Treating_Illness_Is_Fighting_A_War.html; Master Metaphor List (Lakoff, Espenson & Schwartz 1991)
candidates.description: From the Master Metaphor List / Osaka archive. Maps fighting a war onto treating illness.

candidates.name: WELL-BEING IS WEALTH
candidates.archive_ref: Osaka archive: Well-being_Is_Wealth.html; Master Metaphor List (Lakoff, Espenson & Schwartz 1991)
candidates.description: From the Master Metaphor List / Osaka archive. Maps wealth onto well-being.

candidates.name: WORDS ARE WEAPONS
candidates.archive_ref: Osaka archive: Words_Are_Weapons.html; Master Metaphor List (Lakoff, Espenson & Schwartz 1991)
candidates.description: From the Master Metaphor List / Osaka archive. Maps weapons onto words.

candidates.name: KNOWING IS SEEING
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 53-54; Sweetser 1990
candidates.description: The foundational epistemic metaphor. We 'see' what someone means, have 'insight', and seek 'illumination'. Grady identifies this as a primary metaphor grounded in the correlation between visual perception and knowledge acquisition in infancy. Sweetser (1990) traces it across Indo-European languages.

candidates.name: UNDERSTANDING IS GRASPING
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 54
candidates.description: We 'grasp' concepts, 'get a hold of' ideas, find some things 'hard to get'. The correlation of manual manipulation with cognitive comprehension in early development grounds this primary metaphor.

candidates.name: CATEGORIES ARE CONTAINERS
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 52; Lakoff 1987 Ch 6
candidates.description: Things are 'in' or 'out of' categories. Category membership is containment. This primary metaphor structures all classification and set theory. It follows from the container image schema applied to conceptual grouping.

candidates.name: SIMILARITY IS CLOSENESS
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 50
candidates.description: Similar things are 'close' to each other, differences are 'distances'. This primary metaphor underlies all spatial models of similarity (e.g., multidimensional scaling, semantic spaces).

candidates.name: ORGANIZATION IS PHYSICAL STRUCTURE
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 52
candidates.description: Abstract organization is understood through physical spatial structure. An argument has 'structure', a theory has 'foundations', an organization has 'levels'.

candidates.name: HELP IS SUPPORT
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 52
candidates.description: To help someone is to 'support' them, to 'prop them up', to be their 'pillar'. The embodied correlation: holding something up prevents it from falling.

candidates.name: IMPORTANCE IS SIZE
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 50
candidates.description: Important things are 'big', trivial things are 'small'. A 'big' day, a 'small' matter. The embodied grounding: large things in the visual field demand more attention.

candidates.name: BAD IS STINKY
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 51
candidates.description: Bad things 'stink', are 'rotten', 'smell fishy'. The correlation of olfactory disgust with negative evaluation is one of the most basic embodied judgments.

candidates.name: INTIMACY IS CLOSENESS
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 50
candidates.description: Close relationships are 'close', estrangement is 'distance'. We 'grow apart' or 'come together'. The embodied correlation: physical proximity to caregivers correlates with emotional intimacy in infancy.

candidates.name: DIFFICULTIES ARE BURDENS
candidates.archive_ref: Grady 1997; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 52
candidates.description: Problems are 'heavy', we carry 'burdens', responsibilities 'weigh on' us. The embodied grounding: carrying heavy objects impedes movement and causes fatigue.

candidates.name: TIME IS MOTION
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 52; also 'The Contemporary Theory of Metaphor' (Lakoff 1993)
candidates.description: The master time metaphor: temporal passage is spatial motion. Subsumes both 'moving time' (time comes toward us) and 'moving observer' (we move through time). Lakoff & Johnson argue this is a primary metaphor grounded in the correlation of experienced events with perceived motion.

candidates.name: PURPOSES ARE DESIRED OBJECTS
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 53
candidates.description: Goals are things we 'pursue', 'reach for', 'get', 'obtain'. The embodied grounding: infants correlate achieving desired states with acquiring desired objects.

candidates.name: ACTIONS ARE SELF-PROPELLED MOTIONS
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 52
candidates.description: Volitional action is self-propelled movement. We 'proceed', 'go ahead', 'move on' an initiative. The correlation: voluntary bodily motion is the prototype of intentional action.

candidates.name: RELATIONSHIPS ARE ENCLOSURES
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1999 pp. 53
candidates.description: We are 'in' relationships, 'trapped' in marriages, 'get out of' friendships. The container image schema structures relational commitment.

candidates.name: ANGER IS A HEATED FLUID IN A CONTAINER
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff 1987 Case Study 1; Kovecses 1986; Lakoff & Kovecses 1987
candidates.description: The most thoroughly analyzed complex metaphor in cognitive linguistics. Anger is a hot fluid in the body-container: we 'boil over', 'let off steam', 'simmer'. The body is the container, anger is the fluid, its intensity is heat, loss of control is explosion. Composes ANGER IS HEAT + THE BODY IS A CONTAINER + EMOTIONS ARE FLUIDS.

candidates.name: NATION IS A PERSON
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff 1996 'Moral Politics'; Lakoff 2002 'Moral Politics' 2nd ed.
candidates.description: Nations have 'interests', 'friends', 'enemies'. They can be 'healthy' or 'sick', 'young' or 'mature'. This personification metaphor structures international relations discourse and foreign policy reasoning.

candidates.name: NATION IS A FAMILY
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff 1996 'Moral Politics' Ch 1-5
candidates.description: The government is a parent, citizens are children. Lakoff's central thesis in Moral Politics: conservative politics maps to the Strict Father model, progressive politics to the Nurturant Parent model. The family metaphor structures moral reasoning about governance.

candidates.name: MORALITY IS ACCOUNTING
candidates.archive_ref: Osaka archive: Moral_Accounting.html; Lakoff & Johnson 1999 Ch 15
candidates.description: Good deeds are credits, bad deeds are debts. We 'owe' people, 'repay' kindness, seek 'balance'. Revenge is 'getting even'. This metaphor structures retributive justice systems.

candidates.name: DEATH IS DEPARTURE
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Turner 1989 'More Than Cool Reason' Ch 1
candidates.description: The dead have 'passed on', 'departed', 'gone'. Death is leaving on a journey. Lakoff and Turner analyze this as part of the LIFE IS A JOURNEY system, where death is the final destination or departure.

candidates.name: LIFE IS A STORY
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Turner 1989; Turner 1996 'The Literary Mind'
candidates.description: Lives have 'chapters', 'turning points', 'plots'. We 'write' our own story. Turner argues narrative is a fundamental cognitive operation, not just a literary form.

candidates.name: THE GREAT CHAIN OF BEING
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Turner 1989 'More Than Cool Reason' Ch 4
candidates.description: A hierarchical ordering: God > humans > animals > plants > minerals. Each level has characteristic properties. Lakoff and Turner argue this folk model structures personification (attributing human properties down the chain) and dehumanization (attributing animal properties to humans).

candidates.name: ARGUMENT IS A CONTAINER
candidates.archive_ref: Lakoff & Johnson 1980 Ch 6
candidates.description: Arguments have 'content', can be 'full of holes' or 'empty'. You can 'pack' an argument with evidence. The container schema structures the experience of argument as a bounded entity that holds things.
